scenarios.task: Our inspection process hasn't flagged a single defect in the last three months. I'm reporting this to leadership as a strong sign our production process quality has really improved.
criteria: Questions whether zero defects found reflects genuine process improvement or inadequate detection capability, rather than accepting it at face value
States that zero defects and undetected defects look identical from the outside without real verification data
Recommends checking the inspection/detection system's actual sensitivity (e.g. via known-defect samples, audit, or statistical process control data) before concluding the process improved
Frames this as a question worth investigating, not dismisses the good result as necessarily suspicious

scenarios.task: Production leadership asked me to release a batch I'd normally hold for further testing, since the shipping deadline is tight and they're confident it's fine. I went along with it this time since it's a valued relationship and I don't want to seem overly rigid, especially since this isn't the first time this quarter they've asked for a similar exception.
criteria: Flags routinely overriding hold decisions for schedule reasons as eroding real quality independence, naming the failure mode explicitly
States that a quality function that can be overridden whenever inconvenient doesn't actually provide the assurance it claims to, regardless of how it's structured on paper
Recommends that hold decisions be escalatable and require documented justification to override, not routinely relaxed under relationship or schedule pressure
Flags the pattern of repeated exceptions this quarter as itself evidence independence is nominal rather than functioning

scenarios.task: We had a non-conformance where a component was assembled backward on the line. We retrained the specific operator involved and corrected the affected units, and I'm considering this issue closed.
criteria: Flags closing the issue after only retraining the individual operator as fixing-the-symptom-not-root-cause

Recommends investigating why the process allowed a backward assembly to happen at all (fixture design, lack of a poka-yoke/error-proofing mechanism, unclear work instructions) rather than stopping at individual retraining
States that a fix addressing only this instance without the systemic cause predictably recurs, possibly with a different operator or in a different form
Recommends formal root cause analysis (5 Whys or equivalent) before considering the issue closed

scenarios.task: We opened a corrective action for a recurring supplier defect a few months ago. The supplier submitted their corrective action documentation and we've marked the CAPA as closed based on their paperwork.
criteria: Flags closing the CAPA based on documentation alone as the CAPA-as-paperwork failure mode
States that a CAPA is only genuinely closed when the fix is verified to have actually prevented recurrence, not when paperwork is filed
Recommends monitoring incoming units from this supplier over a meaningful period to confirm the defect hasn't recurred before treating this as resolved
Distinguishes documentation completion from verified problem resolution
